# corpus-tool fixture: a short guide to keeping build scripts boring

This document exists only as a golden fixture for the `corpus-tool` test
suite. It is deliberately plain, because the point of a fixture is to be
predictable, not interesting. Below is a short, made-up guide to a
made-up build script, written in the same register a real project README
might use.

A build script has one job: turn source files into artifacts without
surprising anyone. The easiest way to break that promise is to let the
script grow side effects. It starts small. Someone adds a step that
pings a metrics endpoint. Someone else adds a step that writes a cache
file outside the build directory. Six months later, the script cannot
run offline, cannot run twice in a row without manual cleanup, and
nobody remembers why.

The fix is not clever. It is a checklist, applied every time a new step
is proposed. Does the step read anything outside the declared inputs? If
yes, declare the input. Does the step write anything outside the
declared outputs? If yes, declare the output or delete the step. Does
the step depend on the network? If yes, either cache the result and
check it into version control, or gate the step behind an explicit flag
so offline builds still work.

Determinism follows from the same discipline. Two runs with the same
inputs should produce byte-identical outputs. That means no timestamps
baked into generated files, no non-deterministic map or set iteration
order leaking into generated text, and no random seeds unless the seed
itself is derived from the input in a fixed way. Tests that hash the
output of a build step are cheap insurance: if the hash changes without
a corresponding source change, something non-deterministic crept in.

None of this is exciting. That is the point. A build script that nobody
has to think about is a build script that is doing its job. The goal
described here is exactly the kind of goal a corpus of "boring,
competent technical writing" should contain, alongside documents that
are messier, more opinionated, or more personal. Variety in register is
part of what makes a corpus useful for measuring the gap between human
and machine prose, so a document like this one, calm and procedural,
belongs next to others that ramble, argue, or joke.
